skill.name: fp-ts-react
skill.description: Practical patterns for using fp-ts with React - hooks, state, forms, data fetching. Use when building React apps with functional programming patterns. Works with React 18/19, Next.js 14/15.
skill.summary: Functional programming patterns for React using fp-ts - type-safe state, validation, and async handling.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 91 static analysis findings are false positives. The SKILL.md file is documentation containing TypeScript code examples in markdown format. Detected 'external_commands' are markdown backtick code blocks, not shell execution. Network findings reference example URLs in documentation. No executable code or actual security risks present.

content.user_title: Build Type-Safe React Apps with fp-ts Patterns
content.value_statement: Managing state, errors, and async operations in React often leads to bugs from null values or unhandled failures. This skill provides practical fp-ts patterns for type-safe state management, form validation with error accumulation, and reliable async data fetching.
actual_capabilities: Implement Option type for handling potentially missing values in React state
Build form validation with Either type that accumulates all error messages
Create custom hooks using TaskEither for type-safe async data fetching
Apply RemoteData pattern for loading/error/success state management
Use ReaderTaskEither with React Context for dependency injection
Implement React 19 patterns with use(), useActionState, and useOptimistic
limitations: Requires TypeScript and fp-ts library installation in your project
Code examples are documentation patterns, not executable skill commands
Does not provide automated code generation or project scaffolding
Patterns assume familiarity with functional programming concepts

use_cases.title: Form Validation with Error Collection
use_cases.description: Validate signup forms showing all field errors at once instead of one at a time. Use Either with applicative validation to collect errors from email, password, and name fields simultaneously.
use_cases.target_user: React developer building accessible forms with comprehensive validation feedback

use_cases.title: API Data Fetching with Proper State Handling
use_cases.description: Replace boolean flags (isLoading, isError, data) with RemoteData type that enforces exactly four states: NotAsked, Loading, Failure, Success. Eliminates impossible state combinations.
use_cases.target_user: Frontend engineer managing complex async state in dashboards or data-heavy applications

use_cases.title: Type-Safe Optional User Data
use_cases.description: Handle logged-in vs logged-out UI states using Option type instead of null checks. Pattern match on user presence for cleaner conditional rendering without undefined guard clauses.
use_cases.target_user: Developer implementing authentication flows with clear type-level user state tracking

prompt_templates.title: Basic Option Pattern for Missing Values
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to use fp-ts Option type in React to handle a user profile that may not be loaded yet. Include a useState hook and pattern match with O.match for rendering different UI states.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner learning to replace null/undefined with Option type for clearer intent

prompt_templates.title: Form Validation Collecting All Errors
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a signup form validation using fp-ts Either that shows all validation errors at once. Validate email format, password length (8+ chars), and required name field. Use sequenceS to combine validators.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building registration forms that need to display multiple field errors simultaneously

prompt_templates.title: Custom Hook with TaskEither for API Calls
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a useFetch custom hook using fp-ts TaskEither that wraps the fetch API. Handle HTTP errors, loading states, and provide type-safe results. Show how to chain multiple API calls with flatMap.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating reusable data fetching logic with proper error handling for REST APIs

prompt_templates.title: RemoteData State Machine for Complex Async UI
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement the RemoteData pattern for React with four states: NotAsked, Loading, Failure, Success. Create a fold function to pattern match all states and show a complete UserProfile component that fetches data on mount.
prompt_templates.scenario: Replacing multiple boolean flags with a single type-safe state machine for production async UIs

output_examples.input: User submits signup form with invalid email and short password
output: Form displays two error messages together:
- Password must be at least 8 characters
Form data is not submitted until all validations pass

output_examples.input: Dashboard component fetches user profile, stats, and notifications in parallel
output: All three API requests execute concurrently
Success state displays complete dashboard with user info, metrics, and notification list
If any request fails, error state shows with retry option

best_practices: Use useMemo to prevent unnecessary re-renders when creating fp-ts values like O.some() that generate new references each render
Prefer RemoteData over separate loading/error/data booleans to eliminate impossible state combinations at the type level
Use Either with applicative validation (sequenceS) for forms to show all field errors instead of failing on first error
anti_patterns: Do not use fp-ts Option Either TaskEither as React dependency array values directly without memoization - they create new references every render
Avoid mixing null undefined checks with Option in the same codebase - commit fully to fp-ts patterns for consistency
Do not use TaskEither for synchronous operations - reserve it for async operations that might fail, use Either for sync validation

faq.question: What is fp-ts and why use it with React?
faq.answer: fp-ts is a functional programming library for TypeScript that provides types like Option (for missing values), Either (for errors), and TaskEither (for async operations). It brings type safety to common React patterns, catching bugs at compile time instead of runtime.

faq.question: Is fp-ts worth the learning curve for React projects?
faq.answer: For teams comfortable with TypeScript and functional programming, fp-ts reduces runtime errors and improves code clarity. For simpler projects, the added complexity may not be justified. Start with Option and Either before adopting TaskEither and ReaderTaskEither.

faq.question: How does RemoteData improve on useState with loading and error flags?
faq.answer: RemoteData encodes exactly four valid states (NotAsked, Loading, Failure, Success) as a discriminated union. This prevents impossible states like loading=true with error set, which are possible when using separate boolean flags.

faq.question: Can I use fp-ts patterns with React Server Components?
faq.answer: Yes. Option Either and other synchronous fp-ts types work well in Server Components. TaskEither is less relevant since RSCs handle async differently, but the validation patterns with Either remain useful for form handling.

faq.question: What fp-ts React hooks libraries are recommended?
faq.answer: Use fp-ts-react-stable-hooks for referentially stable hooks that understand fp-ts equality. This prevents unnecessary re-renders when using fp-ts values as dependencies. For RemoteData, @devexperts/remote-data-ts provides the type with useful utilities.

faq.question: How do I handle dependency injection with fp-ts in React?
faq.answer: Combine React Context with ReaderTaskEither. Define an AppDependencies interface with your services (API, analytics, etc.), provide it via Context, and use ReaderTaskEither to compose operations that depend on those services while keeping components testable.
